quote: What interest did you in solar, saving some money?
stewart_read: Becky leads the interest question with a yes/no prompt that hands John the answer. He confirms 'saving money' and adds ecology and independence — but only because he volunteered them, not because Becky drew them out. The codex flags this as the emotional anchor of the call and calls for 60+ seconds of depth. Becky spent roughly 10 seconds here and moved on. The closer has John's three stated motivations but no sense of which one is primary or what's driving the urgency — Becky never asked.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Becky was moving efficiently through verify to get to bill collection, planning to anchor emotionally later. Rejected — there is no later emotional anchor in the transcript. The bill is collected as data and the call moves to scheduling.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, play the 01:09–01:22 segment. Ask Becky: 'What did you learn about why John actually wants solar?' She'll cite the three items. Then ask: 'Which one is keeping him up at night?' She won't know. That's the gap. The interest question is the one place in the call where the closer's leverage gets built — Becky treated it as a checkbox.

stewart_read: John said his family has owned the home since 1972 but that he personally has owned it 'a couple years.' Becky records 1972 as the ownership date. This is a data-accuracy error: the verify item is asking about the customer's relationship to the home, not the family's. A customer who has personally owned a home for two years may have different bill-pattern familiarity and different decision-making dynamics than a 50-year owner. The closer will walk in with a misleading data point.
coaching_implication: Brief coaching moment — not a major gap, but worth flagging. Show Becky the exchange and ask what she recorded. When she sees '72, ask her what John actually said. The lesson: when a customer gives a compound answer ('family since X, me for Y'), record the personal ownership date, not the family date. Takes 30 seconds to fix in the 1-on-1.

quote: Well, if you gotta walk me through it a little bit since I'm so it's not a challenge.
stewart_read: John signals immediately that he will need guidance navigating his phone. The codex's elderly_low_tech_protocol calls for a pivot to verbal readback within two minutes of friction. Instead, Becky spends the next 11+ minutes walking John through Samsung Galaxy navigation — share buttons, three-dot menus, screenshot instructions, gallery vs. camera — while John is outdoors, away from home, talking to passersby. The pivot never comes. The bill is eventually sent at 16:48, but the cost is roughly 11 minutes of call time and a customer whose attention has visibly drifted.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Becky knew this was a paid lead requiring the bill before booking and was correctly refusing to skip it. Partially valid — bill collection is required on paid leads. But the codex explicitly provides a verbal readback path as an alternative to photo submission. The issue is not whether to collect the bill; it is that Becky had no fallback path when the photo route stalled.
coaching_implication: This is the call's biggest time-burn. In the 1-on-1, play from 05:13 to 16:48 and ask Becky to estimate how long that segment ran. Then show her the codex's two-minute pivot rule. The coaching question: 'At what point did you know the photo path wasn't going to be fast?' She'll identify it early. Then: 'What else could you have done?' Introduce the verbal readback path — have John read the kWh numbers off the bill aloud. Same data, fraction of the time.

quote: I've I've I've imagined what it would take. It's because of the trees around the house, it's best to have field of solar panels, a 100 feet away from the house on the south end off the south end by about, like, ninety, hundred feet, something like that.
stewart_read: John has not just filled out a form — he has done independent research, thought through the tree-shading problem, identified a ground-mount solution, and picked a cardinal direction. This is a highly pre-qualified customer who has been mentally planning a solar install. Becky's response is 'Okay. I mean, your house, there's not a lot of trees covering your roof' — she redirects to the satellite map rather than anchoring on John's engagement. The enthusiasm signal is walked past entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Becky's satellite check was a legitimate qualification move — she was verifying whether John's tree concern was real. Partially valid, but the two moves are not mutually exclusive. She could have validated his thinking ('You've clearly been thinking about this — that's exactly the kind of detail our specialist loves to work with') before checking the map.
coaching_implication: Show Becky this moment in the 1-on-1 and ask: 'What did John just tell you about himself?' The answer is: he's a motivated, self-researching customer who has already solved his own objection. That's the closer's best opening. Becky's job here was to reflect the enthusiasm back — 'You've already done the homework, that's exactly what our specialist needs' — and use it to build appointment urgency. Instead she pivoted to the map. The closer needs to know John has a ground-mount preference and has thought through the tree issue.

quote: They just, when they call you, they need you to be not driving and able to access your email. Okay?
stewart_read: Becky states the no-driving / at-computer requirement correctly but only once, and John's response is 'I need to be not driving and access the email? Well, I can do it now. I mean, well, I just did it' — a deflection, not a commitment. Becky does not re-anchor. When John explicitly says at 19:50 'I can't guarantee I'm not driving,' Becky accepts it and books. The codex flags this requirement as show-rate predictive. A customer who takes the closer's call while driving or away from a screen is a near-certain no-sit.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Becky judged that pushing harder on the no-driving requirement would risk losing the booking entirely with a customer who is 200 miles from home and clearly mobile. Possible — but the codex does not offer an exception here, and the correct move is to re-anchor the time to when John will be home and settled (Friday evening, at his desk), not to accept a conditional commitment.
coaching_implication: Play the 18:32–19:55 segment. Ask Becky: 'Did John commit to being available for this call?' She'll recognize he didn't. The coaching move: when a customer says 'I can't guarantee I'm not driving,' the rep's response is not to accept it — it's to re-anchor: 'Let's make sure we pick a time when you're home and can pull it up on your computer — that's when you'll actually be able to see the design. Does Friday evening work better than 4PM?' The goal is a committed sit, not a booked time slot.

quote: Okay. Call me back. Put you tomorrow. Put you tomorrow, and then they'll call you at 10AM. Okay?
stewart_read: This is the second of three scheduling pivots in under two minutes. John had already agreed to Friday at 17:42. Becky re-opens the question by proposing same-day (19:04), then tomorrow (19:14), before landing back on Friday at 19:35. Each pivot signals uncertainty and gives John a reason to feel the appointment is not real. The codex requires a confirmed datetime stated once, clearly. Becky's path to Friday 4PM is correct in destination but damaging in execution.
alternative_hypothesis: Becky was trying to accelerate the sit by offering an earlier time — a legitimate tactic if the customer is warm. Partially valid, but John had already said 'I'll be back Friday' at 17:30. Proposing same-day and tomorrow ignores that constraint and creates confusion rather than urgency.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, play the 17:05–19:55 scheduling sequence and ask Becky to count how many times the appointment time changed. The lesson: once the customer names a day, anchor to it immediately and confirm once. 'Friday the 20th at 4PM — I've got you locked in. You'll get a text from me with the details.' No re-opens.

quote: I'm a get you all filled out with your estimated design, and then we'll see you on Friday. Okay?
stewart_read: Becky closes the call without the 'is that fair?' verbal handshake. The codex flags this element as show-rate predictive and marks skipping it as a coaching gap. John's final response is 'Sounds good' — cooperative, but that's John's baseline throughout the call. The handshake is not a formality; it is a micro-commitment that converts a passive 'sounds good' into an active agreement. Its absence here compounds the already-fragile show-rate picture (no-driving non-commitment at 19:50, three scheduling pivots).
alternative_hypothesis: John was cooperative throughout and 'Sounds good' functions as an implicit handshake. Rejected — the codex is explicit that the verbal handshake is required precisely because cooperative customers still no-show. The handshake creates a different psychological commitment than a passive affirmation.
coaching_implication: Quick fix — easiest coaching win on this call. Show Becky the button_up checklist and ask her to walk through what she hit and what she missed. The 'is that fair?' line takes three seconds and is the last thing the customer hears before hanging up. Pair this with the no-driving anchor gap (18:32) — both are button_up discipline issues that compound each other on show rate.
